    20→

    22→
    23→- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos456 ... end Erdos456`.

    26→
    27→## 5. Custom Definitions
    28→
    29→- [x] Helper definitions `smallestPrimeCong1` and `smallestTotientDiv` both have LaTeX docstrings (`/-- ... -/`).
    30→- [x] Both definitions are marked `noncomputable` (they use `sInf` on potentially infinite sets).
    31→- [x] No `sorry` in definitions.
    32→- [x] Functions returning types use `lowerCamelCase` (`smallestPrimeCong1`, `smallestTotientDiv`).
    33→- [x] No local notation used (none needed for this problem).
    34→- N/A: No `@[category test]` / `@[category API]` sanity checks added (optional).
    35→
    36→## 6. Theorem Statements
    37→
    38→- [x] All three statements use the `theorem` keyword.
    39→- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_456` (snake_case, matching namespace number).
    40→- [x] Names follow Lean/Mathlib snake_case conventions.
    41→- [x] Each theorem has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line** (e.g., `@[category research open, AMS 11]`).
    42→- [x] All three theorems have docstrings.
    43→- [x] Docstrings reference source papers (`[Er79e, p.80]`, `[ErGr80, p.91]`).
    44→
    45→### `answer()` usage
    46→
    47→The original problem poses three yes/no questions: "Is it true that $m_n < p_n$ for almost all $n$?", "Does $p_n / m_n \to \infty$…?", "Are there infinitely many primes…?". All three are **Case 1** (yes/no).
    48→
    49→- [x] All three theorems use `answer(sorry) ↔ P`.
    50→- [x] The RHS (`P`) formalizes the question as originally asked in each case, so `answer(True)` would mean "yes".
    51→- [x] Quantifiers appear **after** `answer(sorry)` (in the RHS of `↔`), not as theorem parameters.
    52→
    53→## 7. Category Attribute
    54→
    55→- [x] Every theorem has **exactly one** `@[category ...]`.
    56→- [x] Value is `research open` for all three — appropriate since all three parts are open problems.
    57→- [x] All `research open` theorems contain `sorry` in their proof bodies.
    58→
    59→## 8. AMS Attribute
    60→
    61→- [x] Every theorem has **exactly one** `@[AMS ...]` attribute.
    62→- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed (`11` — Number theory).
    63→- [x] Numbers in ascending numeric order (single entry, trivially satisfied).
    64→- [x] No duplicate numbers.
    65→- [x] `11` is a valid AMS MSC2020 code.
    66→
    67→## 9. Proof Bodies
    68→
    69→- [x] All three open conjectures use `by\n  sorry` (acceptable multi-line form).
    70→- [x] No `sorry` in `def` or `abbrev` declarations.
    71→- [x] No informal proof sketches for these open research problems.
    72→- [x] No `native_decide`.
    73→
    74→## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems
    75→
    76→- [x] Variants are named `erdos_456.variants.ratio_diverges` and `erdos_456.variants.unique_preimage`.
    77→- [x] All variants live in the **same file** as the main theorem.
    78→- [x] Each variant has its own `@[category ..., AMS ...]` attributes.
    79→- [x] Variants are documented with docstrings describing which part of the problem they formalize.
    80→

    82→
    83→- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
    84→- [x] Unicode math symbols used where applicable (`ℕ`, `ℝ`, `↔`, `∀`, `∣`, `≤`, `→`).
    85→- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
    86→- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.

file.content:
# Review: `deepmind/456.lean` — Erdős Problem 456

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line (`import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`), immediately after the copyright block, separated by one blank line.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line.
- [x] Starts with heading: `# Erdős Problem 456`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with markdown link to `erdosproblems.com/456`.
- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of all three parts of the problem.
- [x] Bibliography entries provided: `[Er79e]` (Erdős 1979) and `[ErGr80]` (Erdős–Graham 1980).

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos456 ... end Erdos456`.
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase` (`Erdos456`).
- [x] `open` / `open scoped` statements appear **before** the namespace block (`open Finset Filter Classical`, `open scoped Topology`).

## 5. Custom Definitions

- [x] Helper definitions `smallestPrimeCong1` and `smallestTotientDiv` both have LaTeX docstrings (`/-- ... -/`).
- [x] Both definitions are marked `noncomputable` (they use `sInf` on potentially infinite sets).
- [x] No `sorry` in definitions.
- [x] Functions returning types use `lowerCamelCase` (`smallestPrimeCong1`, `smallestTotientDiv`).
- [x] No local notation used (none needed for this problem).
- N/A: No `@[category test]` / `@[category API]` sanity checks added (optional).

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] All three statements use the `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_456` (snake_case, matching namespace number).
- [x] Names follow Lean/Mathlib snake_case conventions.
- [x] Each theorem has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line** (e.g., `@[category research open, AMS 11]`).
- [x] All three theorems have docstrings.
- [x] Docstrings reference source papers (`[Er79e, p.80]`, `[ErGr80, p.91]`).

### `answer()` usage

The original problem poses three yes/no questions: "Is it true that $m_n < p_n$ for almost all $n$?", "Does $p_n / m_n \to \infty$…?", "Are there infinitely many primes…?". All three are **Case 1** (yes/no).

- [x] All three theorems use `answer(sorry) ↔ P`.
- [x] The RHS (`P`) formalizes the question as originally asked in each case, so `answer(True)` would mean "yes".
- [x] Quantifiers appear **after** `answer(sorry)` (in the RHS of `↔`), not as theorem parameters.

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has **exactly one** `@[category ...]`.
- [x] Value is `research open` for all three — appropriate since all three parts are open problems.
- [x] All `research open` theorems contain `sorry` in their proof bodies.

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has **exactly one** `@[AMS ...]` attribute.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed (`11` — Number theory).
- [x] Numbers in ascending numeric order (single entry, trivially satisfied).
- [x] No duplicate numbers.
- [x] `11` is a valid AMS MSC2020 code.

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] All three open conjectures use `by\n  sorry` (acceptable multi-line form).
- [x] No `sorry` in `def` or `abbrev` declarations.
- [x] No informal proof sketches for these open research problems.
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- [x] Variants are named `erdos_456.variants.ratio_diverges` and `erdos_456.variants.unique_preimage`.
- [x] All variants live in the **same file** as the main theorem.
- [x] Each variant has its own `@[category ..., AMS ...]` attributes.
- [x] Variants are documented with docstrings describing which part of the problem they formalize.

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used where applicable (`ℕ`, `ℝ`, `↔`, `∀`, `∣`, `≤`, `→`).
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (<100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] `lake build` — not verified in this review (requires build environment).
- [ ] Custom linters — not verified in this review (requires build environment).

---

**Verdict:** All statically checkable items **pass**. The file is well-structured, with clean definitions, correct `answer()` usage for all three yes/no sub-problems, proper variant naming, and complete docstrings with paper references. Only build verification remains to be confirmed.

content.text: The review passed — all statically checkable items pass. No changes needed.
